headlineSummary: HN discussion tilted negative for all three providers, with Anthropic drawing the most criticism but the three are in a close tie overall [E1] [E3] [E8].

ranking.summary: OpenAI was also negative, mainly بسبب complaints about hallucinated product suggestions, broken links, privacy concerns, and a shift toward ad-driven monetization and third-party partnerships [E6] [E7] [E8] [E9].
Google Gemini drew limited evidence but it was clearly negative, with one commenter saying Gemini gave wrong and misleading information and they stopped using it [E1].

Anthropic had the heaviest negative reaction, driven by complaints about dumbing down models, tiny quotas, KYC, pricing/value concerns, and restricted tuning, though a few comments were mixed or mildly favorable [E3] [E4] [E10] [E11] [E12] [E13] [E14].

evidence.excerpt: Claude and Gemini failed me more than a couple of times with wrong, misleading and unnecessary information but on the other hand Qwen always gave me proper, practical and correct information. I’ve almost stopped using Claude and Gemini to not to waste my time anymore.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Claude gave wrong and misleading information and they have almost stopped using it.
The commenter directly says Gemini failed them with wrong and misleading information and they have almost stopped using it.

evidence.excerpt: That said, in certain areas such as security-related tasks, I’ve personally had cases where Qwen performed better than Opus. In my personal experience, Qwen tends to perform much better than Opus on shorter units like individual methods or functions. However, when looking at the overall coding experience, I found it works better as a function-level generator rather than as an autonomous, end-to-end coding assistant like Claude.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt criticizes Opus on some tasks but also says Claude is better as an end-to-end coding assistant, making the overall stance mixed.

evidence.excerpt: I pray the benchmark figures are true so I can stop paying Anthropic after screwing me over this quarter by dumbing down their models, making usage quotas ridiculously small, and demanding KYC paperwork.

annotations.rationale: The commenter accuses Anthropic of worsening models, imposing tiny quotas, and requiring KYC, which is strong criticism.

evidence.excerpt: This is the rugpull that is starting to push me to reconsider my use of Claude subscriptions. The "free ride" part of this being funded as a loss leader is coming to a close.

annotations.rationale: Calling the change a rugpull and reconsidering Claude subscriptions is strong negative pricing/value sentiment toward Anthropic.

evidence.excerpt: I'm really surprised that: 1. Anthropic has not published anything about why they made the change and how exactly they changed it

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes Anthropic for not explaining the tokenizer change, indicating dissatisfaction with transparency.

evidence.excerpt: FWIW, I've gone to ChatGPT multiple times with a specific intent to buy, like "hey I need a thing like X or Y, but with quality Z too" and sometimes it just hallucinates things that appear never to have existed, other times it comes up with real items, but the links it gives me to buy them lead nowhere, so I end up just googling the name of it and buying it that way

annotations.rationale: The commenter says ChatGPT hallucinates products and gives broken buying links, which is direct criticism of output quality.

evidence.excerpt: Didn't they explicitly say the ads wouldn't be made aware of prompt data when they announced them? And if so, how is that not securities fraud?

annotations.rationale: In the OpenAI/ChatGPT ads story context, this questions whether prompt data is being used contrary to prior statements, expressing distrust and privacy concern.

evidence.excerpt: What a sad path to see such a bright star going down. I guess it’s not a huge surprise, but it really does paint a bleak picture of technology to see how narrow the range of likely outcomes is. Doesn’t matter if you built the foundation for the future and cured cancer - the most likely outcome is being back to optimizing for engagement and revenue.

annotations.rationale: The commenter laments the move into ad-driven monetization as a sad decline, expressing strong disapproval of OpenAI's direction.

evidence.excerpt: The most surprising thing to me is that they're partnering with third parties to do this. Less secure, lower margins (more middlemen taking fees), harder to access, more likely to not work properly.

annotations.rationale: In the OpenAI ads story context, the commenter criticizes the third-party partnership as less secure and less effective.

evidence.excerpt: That makes AWS effectively irrelevant as a serious inference provider for Kimi, and conveniently pushes users onto Bedrock’s significantly more expensive Anthropic models for comparable performance on agentic tasks.

annotations.rationale: The comment characterizes Anthropic models as significantly more expensive for comparable performance, which is negative pricing sentiment.

evidence.excerpt: After Anthropic, Moonshot is another model provider who restricts tweaking of sampling parameters. I do like the idea of the vendor verifier, though.

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes Anthropic for restricting sampling parameter adjustments.

evidence.excerpt: If you were still looking for evidence that the government designation was political rather technological, here it is. The NSA doesn’t care about day to day temper tantrums of political branches, they have work to do and they will use the best tools available to accomplish that work.

annotations.rationale: Given the title's focus on Anthropic's Mythos, 'the best tools available' implies favorable evaluation of Anthropic's tool quality.

evidence.excerpt: The whole artificial scarcity Anthropic created around Mythos / Glasswing is quite brilliant to be honest (I’m Not saying ethical, just brilliant).

annotations.rationale: The commenter praises Anthropic's strategy as brilliant while also questioning its ethics, yielding mixed sentiment.

evidence.excerpt: On the other hand, Qwen 3.6 is really good. It’s still not close to Opus, but it’s easily on par with Sonnet.

annotations.rationale: Using Opus and Sonnet as the stronger comparison baselines conveys favorable sentiment toward Anthropic's models, especially Opus.
